biography.text:
John Lewis is a writer, educator, and cultural chronicler based in Houston, Texas, he brought with him a Southern sensibility and a deep curiosity about people — what drives them, what they create, and how they connect.

A passionate educator, John has spent years nurturing the minds of others while quietly building a body of written work that reflects his wide-ranging interests and sharp observational voice. His writing explores identity, culture, and the richness of everyday life, often finding the extraordinary in the overlooked.

John is the founder of Decadent Identities, an interview platform dedicated to celebrating artists, thinkers, and creators. The series is guided by a simple but radical premise: that the people around us, and what we make together, are always worth pausing to honor.

shortStories.title: Across the Line
shortStories.text:
It’s drawn with toes. Etched in as straight a line as the rocks allow.
“That line ain’t right,” Eric says, watching her work backwards in the dirt.
“How would you draw it then?” J’Nae replies.
“Like this,” Eric says, proceeding to add a few lines perpendicular to J’Nae’s long
straight one.
“Hold on, hold on,” J’Nae says, “You tried adding all them lines last time. That’s not the
game I’m thinking of.”
“Well, what game is it?” Eric asks, running the palm of his right foot through the lines
he’d just drawn.
“How ‘bout ‘this side, that side.’ You know it? You know it! Start on that end facing me.
No, not on my side on that side,” she says, arms gesturing where she wants Eric to stand.
“I don’t think I remember this game.” Eric says, his nose scrunched up at having to
switch lanes. “But okay, now what”
“Now you say, ‘this or that?’ If I choose ‘this’ then you answer my question and I take
two steps forward. If I choose ‘that’ then when you answer my question, you’ll be the one taking
two steps forward. You ready?”
“What about my questions?”
“You can ask those once we’ve crossed paths, cool? Cool.”

“I don’t think I like this game,” Eric mumbles.
“Well, we’re playing so it’s too late,” J’Nae says sternly.
Eric plays the disappointment into invisible piano keys with his left hand before asking,
“This or that?”
“This,” she says, a smile flowing on her face, “When was the first time you dug for
gold?”
“Black gold or yellow gold?” he replies, eyes going between J’Nae and the sky.
“The kind you use a finger to get,” she says, with an edge of frustration.
“When I was three. I was sitting outside while Mama trimmed the bushes. The wind was
blowing and I sneezed and scratched. My finger had some gold on it after that so I wiped it in the
grass.”
J’Nae takes two steps forward.
“This or that?” Eric says again.
“That,” J’Nae begins, “What’s something you love more than eating?”
Eric cocks his head to the side, his right hand rising to rub the dimple in his chin. Then,
thinking hard on an answer, his fore and middle fingers graze across pressed lips.
 “Hmm, honestly I love it so much that the only thing that could possibly be better would
be getting eaten,” he finally replies, a serious look on his face as he stares at J’Nae.
“This,” she replies curtly.
“But I haven’t asked the question yet?” Eric says, taking two steps forward.

“Doesn’t matter, I was going to say ‘this’ either way. So who cares if you asked?” she
says and Eric shrugs.
“Oh, now you don’t care if the question is asked or not?” he says grinning.
The two face one another. One on this side of the line, the other on that side. Only two
steps keep them from standing shoulder to shoulder.
“What are thinking about right now?” J’Nae asks.
“What you hope to get out of this?” Eric responds.
“This,” she says, stepping quickly to stand beside him. “What’s your birthday going to be
like this year?”
“Silent and beautiful.”
“That answer’s too vague,” J’Nae says, planting her feet in the dirt.
“Take your two steps,” he replies softly.
J’Nae trudges her feet forward through the dirt.
“I want you to ask,” he says in an almost whisper.
“Thisss ooorrrr that?” she sings in melancholic tone.
“That,” Eric responds, “What was I really like?”
J’Nae swiftly turns to her right. The dust kicked up by her sudden movement blurs her
eyes, crosses the line. Each day it was becoming harder to remember.

shortStories.title: An Exchange
shortStories.text:
No matter when he pulled history from the collective unconscious, he always saw it in his
own image. All the characters were real and lived with his complexion.
Like the man who used to shovel horse dung off the main cobblestone fairway, or the one
following Jesus as he went to spread “The Way.” Even the Kings and Queens of old had been his
complexion how he saw it. Because of this he found it hard to change the figures of his dreams
into another complexion for his job.
But, like the trash man with a sensitive nose, he had bills and a life and a wife to support.
Sure, he received little blessings here and there. Like the unopened four-count of toilet paper
he’d picked up off the side of the sidewalk on his way home the other day. Or the random bonus
his company paid to one random worker when they needed to write-off a few hundred before tax
season.
Honestly, he made “enough” money. Did his wife complain until they both had time off
to vacation? Sure. Did they have debts to pay off? Who didn’t? The issue with debt is that it was
holding them up from having kids. Still, without them, he made “enough.” Not enough to talk
about his thoughts regarding his dreams and his work. But enough to occasionally make his wife
smile, which most times made him spread his tightly closed lips just a bit too.
“Yo, Gree!” His buddy said to him from across the long work bench his company had
instead of offices.
“What’s good, Jam?” he replied.

He and Jam sat across from one another at work. To either side of them was a guy of
different complexion. He hated this setup when he first began work. Reminded him of the lunch
tables from elementary school. The ones with the seats attached so that they all had to be put
away together.
“Some things were said by a new hire,” Jam says, fingers typing furiously on his setup.
“Sounds like he had a missing screw then,” Gree replies, his fingers doing the same.
“That’s the thing,” Jam says, leaning forward over his monitor to see Gree’s full face.
Then he looks left and then right before saying, “I heard he used to work for central.”
After saying this Jam went back to typing, his fingers moving even faster now. Gree’s did
much the same.
“You know I head a guy talking the other day,” Gree began without looking up from his
monitor.
“Mhm?” Jam replies.
“He said that there’s still places out there,” Gree continues.
“Mmm?” Jam replies, this time sparing a glance at Gree.
Both of their fingers continued glazing across their individual setups much like those
others around them. Except now, Gree and Jam held each other’s gaze instead of looking into
their monitors.
“Places where what you buy comes from real human hands. Like, you shaking hands at
the checkout because you were just given a couple extra apricots for free,” Gree says.

“Mhm,” Jam replies, understanding.
“Like, everybody rich out there too. But like, it’s because they pay, listen to this, with
coins!”
Just then a supervisor walks by behind Gree.
“Type?” Jam says.
“Type!” Gree responds.
For the next few moments, the sound of struck keys greatly resembled the jangle of a
piggy bank. And they both had piggy banks, near full ones, that they kept in a shared hiding hole.
After work they took their separate routes to their separate homes. Jam was so focused on
getting there he mistook a quarter for a rock and kicked it off the sidewalk and down the hill.
Later, while Gree was sitting at the kitchen table deciding whether or not to start writing
down his dreams, he heard a rock hit his roof. ‘Maybe he could sell rocks for coins,’ he thought.
They’d been hitting his roof for months now.

poems.title: Title
poems.text:
After the rain the garden
holds its breath.
Each leaf a small mirror
for what the sky forgot.

I walk the path you laid
before you left—
stone by stone,
the way back in.

poems.title: Title
poems.text:
Winter light on the table.
Your cup still warm.
I read the newspaper
as if you might return.

The clock marks hours
we no longer share.
I fold the page
and set it down.

interviews.title: Miannah Jabre Lawrence
interviews.text: When looking into glass we ask ourselves for honesty. We have a responsibility to be accountable for the person staring back at us. As we reflect on our inner lives, the thought is called to mind. I am art. The immutable fact, art lives within, revealed via self-reflection. Miannah Jabre Lawrence sees her reflection on the canvas in charcoal, graphite, oil paint, acrylic paint, and mixed media. What started as a fascination with drawing eyes, opening the blinds of the mind, evolved. Human faces are unique in that they hold the soul’s stories. The drawing of eyes grew into griot portraiture, storytelling through faces. Miannah began by telling her own story. Shaping and etching her caricature, her image, her soul, into the canvas. In elementary and middle school, she was introduced and intrigued by the act of creating art. With the help of her art teacher Mrs. Weidel and later artist James Pate she was able to own her practice at a young age. However, the expression of plain faces could only capture the surface of her experience. As her confidence grew, she embarked away from traditional portraits. To show more feeling in the image, she began placing the pieces of the face where they gave deeper meaning. Where they could encompass the innate perspective of the muse in question. Eyes, ears, nose, lips, forehead, head shape, all form a repository of human impressions. Through her artwork, Miannah unveils human experiences as the facets that make us who we are.

interviews.title: Title
interviews.text:
Interviewer: Who are you reading now?

JL: Too many things at once, as always. I just finished rereading James Baldwin's "Sonny's Blues" for probably the twentieth time. I'm also deep into a novel by a young writer from Lagos whose name I probably shouldn't mention until I've finished.

interviews.title: Romita Biswas
interviews.text: Romita Biswas is a multidisciplinary artist whose genuineness and grace towards life has led to her embracing a healing artistic practice. It became a part of who she is and holds importance for how she chooses to navigate the world. Romita’s practice began in dance and art. That childlike confidence in her abilities dulled in high school and was reinvigorated during her approach to college graduation. The stress of finishing brought with it a reminder to breathe, release, create. With a tiny sketchbook in hand Romita began to draw again. Bringing to life images whose value is tied to the size of the page. Not enough to cause stress but big enough to feel pride. In time her confidence gradually cultivated toward the canvas. For Romita, art comes in lapping waves like the ocean. An idea, an outline, of how it will look and what it will represent washes in. Succeeding that comes emotion and the soul of the piece. What it wants to be and how it will manifest itself on the page. Romita creates in tears, laughs, yells, and silent contemplation. The magic is in the moment and when she looks back on each piece, she is transported to those moments in her life represented therein. The other half of her Creative practice, dance, remains a major part of her life as well. Expression through movement has brought with it lasting community and the opportunity to pay homage to her culture. Romita’s creativity does not stop on the canvas or the dance floor, however. An advocate for the progressive use of technology, Romita has combined her creativity and passion for engineering to create “DeltaQ,” a blog catered towards the macro use of sustainable technologies. Through her blog she informs of innovative and eco-friendly ways in which we, as human beings, may tackle a few of the ecological issues prevalent in our present world.  Romita is an artist with a strong desire to see society live towards a direction of longevity. Through art, creative thinking, and community she works tirelessly to shift the needle from “N,” nowhere to “S,” a synergetic future.

interviews.title: Shelby Lynn Ruger
interviews.text: We all wish to be protagonists in our own stories. Extraordinary human beings doing things that challenge the generic plot of what it means to live a successful life. Shelby Lynn Ruger is one such exceptional individual. Since a young age she has been in tune with her authentic self. As someone whose love of the arts originated in music and writing, she found herself looking up to singers. Those such as Lauryn Hill, Juan Luis Guerra, The Weeknd, Justin Bieber and Boys II Men inspired her to express her own voice. Performing covers to their hits nurtured her already blossoming talent for singing. In middle school she expanded her writing practice and learned to share emotions through the power of short stories. When Shelby got to high school, she blended her pen with her vocals to craft songs that captured the hearts and spoke to the souls of her classmates. Ukelele in hand she exposed her emotions in poetic melodies. Serenading the hallways with tunes that told raw depictions of the different aspects of her life at the time. The goal for Shelby has always been to spread love and positivity with her music. Whether that be through tales of overcoming heartbreak, gaining confidence, or simply loving life. Shelby is an artist who believes that the good we put out comes back to us in return. She subscribes to the idea that the only thing we should be loyal to is loving ourselves and being good to others. Genuine and authentic, she welcomes new experiences with a smile and an open mind. She knows her power; her mission is for you to know yours too.

interviews.title: Anne Marie Wells
interviews.text: It is in those moments of deep emotion that we often discover our truth. Anne Marie Wells was in the midst of configuring how she felt about her father having stage four lung cancer when poetry fell upon her like a spotlight from heaven. Before he passed, she was able to share with him her excitement over being newly published in various literary journals. Poetry, easily received and instantly critiqued, felt better to her than those forms of writing that take longer to achieve the same feats. Such as stage play, novel, and memoir writing. Success was more easily achieved in poetry and soon her coping mechanism became an obsession. Anne Marie took steps to further embrace this newfound passion by taking and then teaching classes of poetry as a member of the Community Literature Initiative organization. In front of an audience, she found the affirmation of friends and strangers as the push she needed to accept herself as a poet. Grief, anger, love. The complexity of human connection gifted in succinct lines of meaningful words. With rhythm that shapes the reading with the same feel as a performance piece, Anne Marie brings an audience into the intricacies of her life. It’s as though she is there reading each piece to you, unleashing truth as tears from behind closed eyes. The expression feels natural and relatable to one’s own experience. Her most recent collection, “Survived By,” is a culmination of life during her poetic awakening.  It is a memento to the loss of her father. A raw revealing of the anger and grief she felt during that time. Emotion on par with what is felt at a stumped toe or bumped elbow. There is anguish in the pain and understanding in the frustration. Anne Marie’s poetry speaks to those who feel outcast, providing a space for them to commune with themselves and others who have felt as they do. Anne Marrie’s own community is filled with a variety of individuals who have been drawn to her over the years. Choosing to live and experience the extravagance of each adventure she carries with her. She is an artist who places her truth in words, encouraging others to embrace her sentiment of crying or screaming with honest fervor.

references.title: Sarah Chen, The New York Times Book Review
references.text: Lewis writes with a precision that makes the ordinary feel luminous. Every sentence earns its place.

references.title: Michael Torres, The Paris Review
references.text: A master of the quiet moment—his stories linger long after the last page.

references.title: Elena Vasquez, n+1
references.text: One of the most distinctive voices in contemporary American fiction.

references.title: David Okonkwo, Granta
references.text: Lewis has the rare ability to find the epic within the everyday.
